quote: Hey. This is Carter calling from Ion. How are you?
stewart_read: Carter's intro names the company but provides no context for why he's calling — no reference to a form, a Tesla referral, or any lead source. On a legitimate prospect call, this would be a legitimacy gap. Here it compounds the confusion because the 'customer' is actually an Ion contact trying to figure out why Ion is calling them.
coaching_implication: Use this call to reinforce intro discipline: every outbound call opens with company name, rep name, AND the reason for the call. 'This is Carter from Ion — I'm following up on [specific lead source]' would have surfaced the misdial in the first 10 seconds instead of after a 90-second hold.

quote: I don't know. I've just been past a wide net trying to get this ballpark close of what the cost might be.
stewart_read: The rep is not working a setter script at all — they are personally price-shopping solar across multiple companies. This is a fundamental role confusion: the rep is behaving as a consumer, not a setter. Whether this is a personal inquiry that accidentally routed through the Ion dialer, or a rep who has lost track of their call queue, the behavior is outside any setter framework.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep is deliberately playing naive to gather competitive pricing intel for Ion. Rejected — there is no evidence of any structured intel-gathering intent, and the rep's confusion about which company they'd reached is genuine.
coaching_implication: Manager needs to determine how this call entered the queue. If the rep was using Ion's dialer for a personal inquiry, that is a conduct issue, not a coaching issue. If this is a genuine queue misdial, the rep's response ('I don't know... I've been texting people') reveals they have no call-prep discipline — they should know the lead source before dialing.

quote: I'm not sure. I've been texting people. I don't know if it's your company or a different one. No.
stewart_read: The rep cannot identify the lead source or even confirm which company they've been contacting. On a legitimate call, this would be a critical legitimacy failure — the customer's first trust question ('how did you get my number / who are you?') is answered with confusion. The codex requires honest specificity; the rep delivers honest uncertainty, which is worse.
coaching_implication: Pull this clip for the floor as a negative example of call prep. Every rep should be able to state, before dialing: (1) the customer's name, (2) the lead source, (3) the reason for the call. If a rep cannot answer those three questions before the call connects, they should not be dialing.

quote: He'll probably just set you up an appointment to talk to one of our specialists who will go over the pricing for batteries.
stewart_read: The Ion contact mentions 'pricing for batteries' — this is the only product signal in the call. The rep does not engage with it at all, responding only with 'Okay.' On a real prospect call, a customer expressing interest in battery pricing would be a direct cross-sell signal (Tesla Powerwall) and a qualification anchor. Here the rep has already been identified as a misdial, so engagement would be inappropriate — but the manager should note that the rep showed zero instinct to engage even when a product signal was handed to them.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep correctly stayed silent because they understood the call was over and engaging further would be inappropriate. Plausible, but the rep's passivity throughout the call suggests this was not a deliberate strategic choice.
coaching_implication: Low-priority coaching note given the call context, but worth flagging in a broader pattern review: does this rep engage with product signals when they appear on legitimate calls? The battery mention here is a data point, not a coaching priority.
